back_translation: 'Do you know when the mountain goats give birth? Have you watched the doe bear her young?
Can you count the months they carry their young? Do you know the time they give birth?
They crouch down and bring forth their young, ridding themselves of their labor pains.
Their young grow strong and mature in the open field; they leave and do not return.
Who set the wild donkey free? Who loosed the bonds of the swift donkey,
to whom I gave the wilderness as its home and the salt flats as its dwelling?
It scorns the tumult of the city and does not heed the shouts of the driver.
It roams the mountains for its pasture, searching for every green thing.
Will the wild ox consent to serve you? Will it stay by your manger at night?
Can you bind the wild ox to the furrow with a rope? Will it plow the valleys behind you?
Can you rely on its great strength? Will you leave your hard labor to it?
Can you trust it to bring back your grain and gather it to your threshing floor?
The wings of the ostrich flap with joy, but they cannot match the wings and feathers of the stork.
For she leaves her eggs on the ground and lets them warm in the sand,
and she forgets that a foot may crush them or a wild animal may trample them.
She treats her young harshly, as if they were not hers, with no concern that her labor was in vain,
because God has not given her wisdom and has not granted her understanding.
Yet when she spreads her wings and runs proudly, she scorns the horse and its rider.
Do you give the horse its strength? Do you clothe its neck with a mane?
Do you make it leap like a locust? Its proud snorting strikes terror.
It paws the ground in the valley and rejoices in its strength; it charges into battle.
It laughs at fear and is afraid of nothing; it does not turn back from the sword.
The quiver rattles at its side, along with the flashing spear and lance.
Trembling with excitement, it devours the distance and cannot stand still at the sound of the ram's horn.
At the blast of the horn it snorts with fervor; it catches the scent of battle from afar, the shout of captains and the war cry.
Does the hawk take flight and spread its wings toward the south by your understanding?
Does the eagle soar high and make its nest on the heights at your command?
It dwells and lodges on a cliff, on a rocky crag as its stronghold.
From there it searches out food; its eyes see it from afar.
Its young feast on blood, and wherever the slain are, there it is.'
